title.emphasis: Part III: The Condemnation of Egotism and Libertarianism: The Violence of Independence

section.para: The ideologies of egotism and libertarianism take the flawed normative subject of Humanism and radicalize its defining feature: autonomy. By elevating the "myth of independence" to the status of a core political and moral principle, these philosophies promote social structures that are not only in direct violation of biological reality but also legally and ethically indefensible. They are not merely proposing a different way to organize society; they are proposing a way to organize society that is in direct opposition to the fundamental biological and social strategies that have allowed the human species to thrive. Their implementation is an act of systemic self-harm.

title.emphasis: The Anti-Social Costs of the Self-Sufficient Individual

section.para: Libertarianism and egotism are scientifically illiterate ideologies. Their foundational belief in the autonomous, self-sufficient individual as the primary unit of social analysis is directly contradicted by the biological evidence of interdependence presented in Part I. The symbiotic nature of the gut-brain axis, the heritability of epigenetic trauma, and the fundamental reality of mutual reliance for survival all serve as a direct scientific refutation of the libertarian subject.2 Enacting these ideologies creates social structures that are inherently fragile, maladaptive, and psychologically damaging.

A key mechanism of this damage is the erosion of communal care networks. Libertarian social models, with their emphasis on radical individualism and the primacy of the contractual relationship, systematically devalue the non-contractual bonds of community and mutual aid. This is reinforced by a pervasive social norm critiqued by philosophers as "amatonormativity"—the widespread assumption that an exclusive, long-term, amorous relationship is a universal goal and is superior to all other relationship types.2 This norm creates a relationship hierarchy that privileges the romantic, marital couple above all other forms of connection, such as friendships and broader community bonds.2 This has tangible legal and economic consequences, from the denial of benefits afforded to married couples to the social exclusion of those who organize their lives around other forms of kinship.2

The consequence of this dual assault—from a political ideology that champions individualism and a social ideology that devalues non-romantic bonds—is the privatization of care and the isolation of individuals. This atomization is not only socially detrimental but psychologically dangerous. As the research on coercive persuasion demonstrates, social isolation is a key precondition for manipulation and the breakdown of individual identity.6 A society that systematically dismantles its networks of mutual support is a society that produces psychologically vulnerable individuals, primed for exploitation. Libertarianism is therefore a social technology that actively works to dismantle the evolved, biological systems of cooperation upon which human societies depend. It is an ideology at war with our biology.

title.emphasis: A Legal Critique of Procreative Liberty

section.para: The most radical expression of libertarian and egoist philosophy is the concept of absolute bodily autonomy, which extends to an unconditional right to procreative liberty. This position is legally and ethically indefensible because it willfully ignores the non-consensual imposition of profound and foreseeable harm on a third party: the person being created. When analyzed through a legal lens of negligence and a bio-existentialist lens of consent, the act of procreation constitutes a "foundational violation".11

This argument reframes procreation not as a gift or a natural right, but as a planned action by creators that knowingly and unilaterally subjects a non-consenting being to a high-risk condition.11 From a bio-existentialist perspective, this "Initial Violation" is the imposition of
facticity: the unchosen, inescapable material situation of one's existence, including one's genetic code, historical moment, and the guarantee of suffering and death.11

This philosophical critique is given legal force through the principle of "due diligence." In a world with robust and widely available reproductive freedoms, including contraception, abortion, and advanced genetic screening technologies, the choice to proceed with unexamined creation is transformed. It is no longer a neutral act but one that can be analyzed as a form of culpable negligence.11 The availability of these tools constitutes a toolkit for mitigating foreseeable harm. The choice not to utilize them, or to proceed with creation in the face of known high risks (such as heritable disease or epigenetic trauma), represents a gross failure of the creators' primary duty of care to the foreseeable child.11 The creators' ignorance is not a defense; it is an admission of negligence.

This framework directly attacks the core of libertarianism and egotism. It weaponizes the language of individual rights—specifically, the right of the created not to be subjected to non-consensual harm—to dismantle the ideology of absolute individual liberty. It reveals a fatal internal contradiction: the exercise of the creators' absolute liberty results in the absolute violation of the created's autonomy. The libertarian framework, which is blind to this foundational imposition of harm, is therefore exposed as ethically incoherent and legally negligent.
